Facts.text: Anti-pope Clement crowns German King Hendrik IV as Holy Roman Emperor
France & Spain sign truce
Pope Urbanus VIII and Duke of Parma sign Peace of Ferrara
Great earthquake at Cuzco, Peru
France & England sign anti-Dutch military accord
Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I and King John III Sobieski of Poland sign a covenant against Turkey, beginning of the Holy League
A sermon on "The Nature of the Kingdom of Christ" by Benjamin Hoadly, the Bishop of Bangor, provokes the Bangorian Controversy
Bellevue Hospital founded in a New York City almshouse - 1st public hospital in the US
Jews are expelled from Prague
French created Kingdom of Westphalia orders Jews to adopt family names
Massacre of the population of the Greek island of Chios by soldiers of the Ottoman Empire following a rebellion attempt, (later depicted by artist Eugène Delacroix)
Mainzer Rijnvaart Convention ends
Quebec and Montreal are incorporated
Colonel John W Geary arrives as 1st postmaster of San Francisco
US population hits 23,191,876 (Black population: 3,638,808 (15.7%))
Treaty of Kanagawa: Commodore Perry forces Japan to opens ports to US
Confederacy takes over mint at New Orleans (US Civil War)
Civil War action at Island #10 on Mississippi River
Battle of Grand Gulf, Mississippi and Dinwiddie Court House, Virginia
Battle of Boydton, Virginia (White Oaks Roads, Dinwiddie C H)
Confederate Army General Pickett moves to fortify Five Forks in Dinwiddie County, Virginia, abandoning the defense of Petersburg to do so
The Spanish navy bombards the harbor of Valparaíso, Chile
Chinese Embassy arrives aboard steamship China

Thomas Mundy Peterson of Perth Amboy, New Jersey, is the first African American to vote in the US under the provisions of the 15th Amendment to the Constitution in a local election on the town's charter
British High Commissioner Sir Bartle Frere arrives in Capetown
The family with samurai antecedents who responded to the Saigo army in Ōita Nakatsu rebels.
1st town to claim to be completely illuminated by electric lighting (Wabash, Indiana)
1st performance of Caesar Franck's symphonic poem for orchestra "Le Chasseur Maudit" (The Accursed Huntsman) at the Salle Érard in Paris
Utrecht, Belgium begins water pipe system
Great Britain declares Bechuanaland a protectorate
At " Surprise of Sanna’s Post" in the Second Boer War 150 troops under Brigadier General Robert Broadwood are killed
Richard Pearse flies a monoplane several hundred yards in New Zealand
Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States is founded to set rules in amateur sports; becomes the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) in 1910
Romanian Army puts down Moldavian farmers' revolt
In a diplomatic note to Austria, Serbia recognizes the Bosnian annexation and promises to maintain friendly relations with Austria
National Baseball Commission rules players who jump contracts to be suspended for 5 years; players joining outlaw organizations suspended for 3 years
Dutch government ends all military engagements
The Danish West Indies are officially ceded to the US for $25 million and renamed the Virgin Islands
1st daylight savings time in US goes into effect
Strike against Ruhrgebied government of Scheidemann
British Parliament accepts the Government of Ireland Act, known as the Fourth Home Rule Bill
British coal miners goes on strike
The Royal Australian Air Force is formed
KFI-AM in Los Angeles CA begins radio transmissions
Prince Hendrik opens trade fair building in Amsterdam
First dance marathon in New York City; Alma Cummings sets record of 27 hours with 6 different partners
Occupying French soldiers fire on workers when surrounded at Krupp auto factory in Essen, Germany; 6 die, dozens wounded
Stanley Cup Final, Denman Arena, Vancouver, BC: Ottawa Senators (NHL) edge Edmonton Eskimos (WCHL), 1-0 for a 2-0 series sweep
Croydon Airport, London: Imperial Airways established
London public transport strike ends
WOWO-AM, Ft Wayne Indiana begins radio transmission (500 watts)
German Special Court of Justice for state security disbands
The Motion Pictures Production Code is instituted, imposing strict guidelines on the treatment of sex, crime, religion and violence in film for the next 38 years
Brilliant Notre Dame football coach Knute Rockne (43) is killed with 7 others when light plane crashes on trip from Kansas City to Los Angeles; record 105-12-5 @ .881 remains best ever
150 wild swans die in Niagara Falls
Ford publicly unveils its V-8 engine
"Soperton News" publishes 1st newspaper on pine pulp paper, in Soperton, Georgia
US Congress authorizes Civilian Conservation Corps
Netherlands Indies BC Ltd begins radio transmission (Indonesia)
Britain & France agree to support Poland if invaded by Germany
Karelo-Finnish SSR becomes 12th Soviet republic (until 1956)
Ground broken for Union Square Garage, San Francisco
US errantly bombs Rotterdam, kills 326
Hungary orders all Jews to wear yellow stars
3rd Algerian division crosses the Rhine
Sicherheitsdienst murders 10 political prisoners in Zutphen
US artillery lands on Keise Shima/begins firing on Okinawa
Belgian government of Acker forms
Belgian government of Spaak resigns - shortest ever Belgian government
First election is held in Greece after World War II
US Congress passes Marshall Aid Act to rehabilitate war-torn Europe
Newfoundland becomes Canada's 10th province

RCA Victor of Camden, New Jersey, introduces the 45 RPM record player and the 7-inch single, a vinyl disc more durable than the 78 RPM shellac; initial releases include: Eddy Arnold's "Texarkana Baby"; Spade Cooley's "Spanish Fandango"; Arthur Crudup's "Crudup's After Hours"; and Saul Meisel's "A Klein Melamedl"
US tanks exceed 38° of latitude in Korea
US Department of Health, Education & Welfare established
USSR offers to join NATO
Merger of Chase National Bank (3rd largest bank) and Bank of the Manhattan Company (15th largest bank) forms Chase Manhattan
West Indies cricket all-rounder Collie Smith scores 104 in his Test debut in a 9 wicket 1st Test defeat to Australia in Kingston, Jamaica
US Navy forms atomic submarine division
USSR suspends nuclear weapons tests, & urges US & Britain to do same
First Nations people first granted the right to vote in Canada, without giving up their Indian status, in repeal of part of Canada Elections Act
Gore Vidal's play "Best Man" premieres in NYC (Melvyn Douglas - Tony for Best Actor)
Aklilou Habtewold becomes 1st premier of Ethiopia
LA ends streetcar service after 90 years
Iberia Airlines Convair 440 crashes into the sea on approach to Tangier killing 47 of 51 occupants
25,000 anti war demonstrators march in NYC
USSR launches Luna 10, 1st lunar orbiter
Pirate Radio Station Pegaus (NZ) begins transmitting
Seattle's AL club is named Pilots
US PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonauthorizes a troop surge in Vietnam, bringing the total number of US soldiers to a peak of 549,500
Explorer 1 re-enters the Earth's atmosphere after 12 years in orbit
Federal bankruptcy court allows Seattle Pilots to be sold to Milwaukee
Following an Orange Order parade, intense riots erupt on the Springfield Road in Belfast; violence lasts for three days, and the British Army use CS gas for the first time in large quantities
South Africa national debt hits 5.45 billion
William Calley sentenced to life for Mi Lai Massacre
Official Beatles Fan Club closes down
Philadelphia Flyers score an NHL record tying 8 goals in the 2nd period of a 10-2 win over the visiting New York Islanders at the Spectrum
Cleveland Cavaliers beat Jazz, 110-101 in New Orleans to clinch club's first ever NBA playoff berth
NJ Court rules Karen Anne Quinlan may be disconnected from respirator
Michael Cristofer's play "Shadow Box" premieres in NYC (Tony award for Best Play 1977)
USSR launches Kosmos 1000 navigational satellite
Wings release "London Town" album
Montreal Canadiens beat visiting Pittsburgh Penguins, 5-3 to become just the 2nd team in NHL history to win 50 (or more) games for 4 straight years
The last British soldier leaves the Maltese Islands. Malta declares its Freedom Day (Jum il-Helsien).
The Chicago, Rock Island and Pacific railroad operates its final train after being ordered to liquidate its assets due to bankruptcy and debt owed to creditors.
1st Golden Raspberry Awards: "Can't Stop the Music" wins
American rock group The Doobie Brothers split up
Arkas tanker at Montz La, spills 1.47 million gallons of oil
NBA and Players Association reach a 4-year agreement that includes a 53% revenue-sharing plan in return for minimum & maximum payrolls; first of its kind in team sports
Marsha Norman's "'night, Mother" premieres in NYC
Popayán Earthquake (5.5 Mw Depth) in Colombia kills 267 people, injuring some 7,500
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
15th Easter Seal Telethon raises $27,400,000
El Salvador's President José Napoleón Duarte' Christian Democratic Party wins election
English Hampton Court palace badly damaged by fire, 1 dead
Mexicana Airlines Boeing 727 crashes, killing 167
Six metropolitan county councils are abolished in England.
Last East Limburg coal mine closes in Gent Belgium
Dionisio Castro cycles world record 20km (57:18.4)
Major riots occur in London and other British towns in protest against the new Community Charge poll tax laws
Albania offers 1st multi-party election in 50 years
Former "Partridge Family" star Danny Bonaduce attacks a transvestite prostitute in Phoenix, Arizona
Georgian SSR votes on whether to remain in the Soviet Union
Soviet Rep of Georgia endorsed independence; Warsaw Pact dissolves
The Establishment of Islamic Constitutional Movement - Hadas in Kuwait.
Phoenix coach Cotton Fitzsimmons guides the Suns to a 128-111 home win over Portland to become the 6th coach in NBA history to record 800 career wins
Ranji Trophy Cricket Final, Feroz Shah Kotla, Delhi: Delhi (Ajay Sharma 175 in 532) beats Tamil Nadu (449) on 1st innings
UN Security Council voted to ban flights & arms sales to Libya
Actor James Farentino pleads no contest to stalking ex-girlfriend Tina Sinatra
The Journal Nature reports the finding in Ethiopia of the first complete Australopithecus afarensis skull
Walkway from Cleveland's Tower City to Jacobs Field officially opens
Federal judge Sonia Sotomayor of US District Court in NYC issues an injunction against Major League Baseball owners in move that effectively ends 232-day strike
Ranji Trophy Cricket Final, Wankhede Stadium, Bombay: Bombay (Sanjay Manjrekar 224 in 6/690) beats Punjab (Vikram Rathour 177 in 372) on 1st innings
First time in MLB history, the regular season opens in March with Seattle Mariners beating Chicago White Sox, 3-2 in 12 innings at the Kingdome, Seattle
Radio Canada International's final shortwave broadcast
Ranji Trophy Cricket Final, MA Chidambaram Stadium, Madras: Karnataka (Vijay Bharadwaj 146 of 8/620) beats Tamil Nadu (370) on 1st innings
Space Shuttle STS 76 (Atlantis 16), lands
"Daytime to Remember" a series showing old soaps premieres on ABC-TV
Expansion clubs, Tampa Bay and Arizona both suffer bad losses in their MLB debuts; Devil Rays lose, 11-6 to the Detroit Tigers and the Diamondbacks fall, 9-2 to Colorado Rockies
Milwaukee becomes first team since the inception of the American League in 1901 to switch MLB leagues; Brewers lose first NL game, 2-1 to the Braves in Atlanta
Australian swimmer Lisbeth Lenton breaks Inge de Bruijn's 4-year old 100m freestyle women's world record in 53.66s at the Australian Olympic trials in Sydney
In Fallujah, Iraq, 4 American private military contractors working for Blackwater USA, are killed and their bodies mutilated after being ambushed
With a 12-1 defeat of the Tampa Bay Devil Rays in Tokyo, NY Yankees starter Kevin Brown becomes only the 2nd pitcher in MLB history to have beaten all 30 teams
In Sydney, Australia, 2.2 million people take part in the first Earth Hour.
Aloha Airlines, a bankrupt airline, permanently ends passenger service
"Boom Boom Pow" single released by The Black Eyed Peas (Grammy Award for Best Music Video 2010, Billboard Song of the Year 2009)
Fiji Floods kill 2 people and force thousands to be evacuated
11 people are killed in flooding at Port Louis, Mauritius
14 Boko Haram suspects are killed in a Nigerian Army raid
2 people die from bird flu (type H7N9) in China
Mudslides caused by heavy rains sweep through Mocoa, Colombia killing more than 200
Rapper and activist Nipsey Hussle fatally shot outside his clothing store in Los Angeles
British pensioner Robert Weighton becomes the world's oldest man at 112 years
Alfred Aho and Jeffrey Ullman win computing's Turing Award for their work inventing computer program compilers
US PresidentJoe Bidenunveils the "American Jobs Plan," one of the largest infrastructure plans in US history, worth $2 trillion
First truly complete sequence of a human genome published by Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) consortium, after breakthroughs in new technology (previously just over 90% coded)
US announces it will release 1 million barrels of oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve for six months to drive down prices
Large storm system crosses the mid US from Mississippi and Alabama to Illinois and Wisconsin, unleashing numerous tornadoes and leaving at least 32 dead
SpaceX mission Fram2 launches four people aboard a Falcon 9 rocket, becoming the first crewed spaceflight to enter a polar retrograde orbit
Bernard of Clairvaux preaches his famous sermon in a field at Vézelay, urging the necessity of aSecond Crusade. Louis VII andEleanor of Aquitaineare present and join the Crusade.
QueenIsabella I of Castileand KingFerdinand II of Aragonissue the Alhambra Decree expelling Jews from their kingdoms
Pope Alexander VI forms the anti-French alliance the League of Venice with Archduke of AustriaMaximilian I,Ferdinand II of Aragon, Duke of MilanLudovico Sforzaand the Republic of Venice

First Mass held in the Philippines, generally believed to be on the Island of Limasawa in the Archipelago of St Lazarus byFerdinand Magellanand 50 of his men. They take possession of the island the same day.
Henry IIsucceedsFrancis Ias King of France
English Parliament presents the Humble Petition and Advice to Lord ProtectorOliver Cromwell, offering him the crown, but he declines
Immanuel Kantis appointed Professor of Logic and Metaphysics at the University of Königsberg
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's dramatic play "Egmont" premieres in Weimar
Forces allied againstNapoleoncapture Paris
First performance ofRobert Schumann's Symphony No. 1 in B-flat major
Test cricket debut of feared Australian fast bowler Fred "The Demon"Spofforth; Australia loses the second Test by four wickets to England at the Melbourne Cricket Ground
EmperorWilhelm IIof Germany visits Tangier, Morocco and proclaims Germany's support of Moroccan independence and equal opportunity for all powers to trade there
George Bernard Shaw's 1898 play "Caesar and Cleopatra" premieres in a German version in Berlin, German Empire
Gustav Mahlerconducts New York Philharmonic for his 1st time
"The Hound of Baskervilles" first of 14 films starringBasil Rathboneas Sherlock Holmes and Nigel Bruce as Dr. Watson is released
Tennessee Williams' stage drama "The Glass Menagerie" premieres in NYC

William Grant Still's opera "Troubled Island" with libretto byLangston Hughesand Verna Arvey, premieres at the New York City Opera; 1st grand opera composed by an African American produced by a major company
UN Security Council nominatesDag Hammarskjöldfor Secretary-General
Rodgers & Hammerstein's live television musical "Cinderella", starringJulie Andrews, with Kaye Ballard and Alice Ghostley, premieres on CBS-TV
Labour Party underHarold Wilsonwin British parliamentary election
Jimi Hendrixfirst burns his guitar (and his fingers) at Finsbury Park Astoria theatre in London; the guitar is sold at auction for nearly $500K, the building is currently a church
US PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonannounces in an address to the nation that he will not seek re-election
George HarrisonandPattie Boydare fined £250 each for possession of illegal drugs
NY Times reviews "Slaughterhouse-Five" byKurt Vonneguton its publication, saying "you'll either love it, or push it back in the science-fiction corner"
American tennis starBillie Jean Kingends AustralianMargaret Court's 57-match winning streak 6-7, 7-6, 6-3 in semi finals of the WTA event in Indianapolis, IN
Boston Bruins defencemanBobby Orrscores his 3rd career hat trick in a 7-3 loss at Toronto to become the first player in NHL history to score 100 points for 4 straight seasons
Muhammad Alisuffers a broken jaw in a shock split-points decision loss toKen Nortonover 12 rounds in San Diego; Ali wins rematch in another controversial split decision
American golferLee Trevinogoes bogey-free for the full 4 rounds as he wins the Greater New Orleans Open by 8 strokes from Bobby Cole andBen Crenshaw
Almost concurrent heavyweight boxing championship fights:Larry HolmesTKOs Leroy Jones in 8 for WBC title in Las Vegas, NV whilstMike WeaverKOs John Tate in 15 for WBA belt in Knoxville, TN
US PresidentJimmy Carterderegulates banking industry
53rd Academy Awards: "Ordinary People",Robert De Niro&Sissy Spacekwin
"Monty Python's The Meaning of Life," is released in the US
Mike Bossybecomes first player in NHL history to record 7 straight 50 goal seasons; scores 50th and 51st of the year in a 3-1 New York Islanders' win at Washington
New York Islanders celebrate defensemanDenis Potvin's 1,000 points and 1,000 NHL games at tribute night at Nassau Coliseum
Pulitzer prize awarded toToni Morrisonfor her novel "Beloved"
Donald Trumppurchases Eastern's Northeast Shuttle
"Carol & Company" starringCarol Burnettpremieres on NBC-TV
20-year old Quebec Nordiques' centerJoe Sakicscores a goal in a 3-2 loss to Hartford to become the youngest player in NHL history to score 100 points in a season
Robert Wilson,Tom Waits, andWilliam S. Burroughs' avant garde opera "The Black Rider" premieres March 31, 1990 at the Thalia Theater, Hamburg, Germany
St. Louis Blues' future Hockey Hall of Fame right wingBrett Hullscores his 86th goal of the season in a 2-1 win over Minnesota North Stars; 3rd best total in NHL history
Chicago White Sox assigns former NBA superstarMichael Jordanto the Birmingham Barons of Class AA Southern League; returns to NBA after one season
West Indies cricketers win 3rd Test at Bridgetown, Barbados by 38 runs after setting India a modest 120 run target; tourists dismissed for 81 inBrian Lara's debut as Windies' captain
"10 Things I Hate About You", a modernization ofShakespeare's The Taming of the Shrew, starringJulia StilesandHeath Ledger, is released
German brothers Michael andRalf Schumacherbecome first siblings to share front row of the grid in a Formula 1 World Championship event; qualify 1st and 2nd respectively for Brazilian GP in São Paulo
Andre Agassiwins his 700th career match and captures his second straight Key Biscayne title with a 6–3, 6–3, 3–6, 6–4 win overRoger Federerat the Nasdaq-100 Open in Florida
Cincinnati Reds open their new home, The Great American Ball Park, with a 10-1 loss to the Pittsburgh Pirates; former PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushthrows first ceremonial pitch
Elton JohnjoinsGeorge Clooney's call to boycott hotels owned by the Sultan of Brunei after Brunei plans new anti-gay laws to make homosexual sex punishable by death
French PresidentEmmanuel Macronannounces a new pandemic lockdown, closing schools for three weeks with new national restrictions
New York state legalizes recreational use of marijuana in legislation signed by GovernorAndrew Cuomo(sales not legal for 18 months)
Paul Simonsells his entire songwriting catalog of over 400 songs to Sony Music Publishing for an undisclosed sum
US PresidentJoe Bidenoverturns Trump's restrictions on transgender people serving in the armed forces
France's far-right leaderMarine Le Penis found guilty of embezzlement of EU funds, barring her from running for office for five years

Eiffel Tower officially opens for dignitaries and an award ceremony in Paris, France; designed byGustave Eiffeland built for the Exposition Universelle, at 300 meters high, it holds the record for the tallest man-made structure for 41 years
Final day of the rum ration in the Royal Canadian Navy
Constantius Chlorus, Roman Emperor (305-306 AD), born in Dacia Ripensis, Northen Balkans (d. 306)
Philippa of Lancaster, Queen of Portugal as wife of King John I (1387-1415), born in Leicester Castle, Leicester, England (d. 1415)
Bianca Maria Visconti, Duchess of Milan (1450-68), born in Settimo Pavese, Lombardy, Italy (d. 1468)
Pius IV [Gianangelo de' Medici], Italian lawyer and Pope (1559-65), born in Milan (d. 1565)
Guru Angad Dev, second Sikh guru, born in Matte Di Sarai, Muktsar, Panjab (d. 1552)
Ashikaga Yoshiteru, 13th Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1545-65), born in Japan (d. 1565)
Louise Juliana of Nassau, countess consort of the Palatinate by marriage to Frederick IV, Elector Palatine of the Rhine, born in Delft, Habsburg Netherlands (d. 1644)
Andrew Marvell, English poet (To His Coy Mistress), born in Winestead, England (d. 1678)
Charles II, German Elector Palatine, born in Heidelberg (d. 1685)
Benedict XIV [Prospero Lorenzo Lambertini], Italian Pope (1740-58), born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1758)
Francesco Durante, Neapolitan composer, and teacher, born in Frattamaggiore, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1755)
Florian Wrastill, Austrian composer of sacred music, choir master, and Benedictine monk, born near Königgrätz, Bohemia [now Hradec Králové, Czech Republic] (d. 1758)
Mariana Victoria of Spain, Queen consort of Portugal (1750-77), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1781)
Frederick V, King of Denmark-Norway (1746-66), born in Copenhagen Castle, Copenhagen (d. 1766)
Étienne Bézout, French mathematician best known for his theorem involving polynomial equations, born in Nemours, France (d. 1783)
Johann Abraham Peter Schulz, German composer (The Moon Has Risen), born in Lüneburg, Electorate of Hanover, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1800)
Charles Cagniard de la Tour, French engineer and physicist (inventor of the disk siren), born in Paris, France (d. 1859)
Coenraad Jacob Temminck, Dutch aristocrat, zoologist and museum director, born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1858)[1]
Franciszek Wincenty Mirecki, Polish composer, conductor and pedagogue, born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1862)
Thomas M. T. McKennan, American politician who served as the 2nd United States Secretary of the Interior for only 11 days (1850), born in New Castle, Delaware (d. 1852)
J. Pinckney Henderson, American lawyer and politician (1st Governor of Texas, 1846-47; US Senator from Texas, 1857-58), born in Lincolnton, North Carolina (d. 1858)
Edward FitzGerald, British poet, writer, and translator (Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam), born in Bredfield, Sussex, England (d. 1883)
Nikolai Gogol [Nikolay], Ukrainian-born Russian novelist, humorist, and dramatist (Dead Souls, The Inspector-General), born in Sorochintsy, Poltava Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1852)
Otto Jonas Lindblad, Swedish composer (Kungssången - Royal Anthem), born in Karlstorp, Sweden (d. 1864)

Chlodwig Carl Viktor, German Prince of Hohenlohe-Schillingsfürst, Prince of Ratibor and Corvey, Prime Minister of Bavaria, and Chancellor of the German Empire, born in Rotenburg an der Fulda, German Confederation (d. 1901)[1]
Rafael Hernando, Spanish composer of zarzuelas, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1888)
Ernest Doudart de Lagrée, French naval officer and entomologist (led the Mekong River expedition 1866-68), born in Saint-Vincent-de-Mercuze, France (d. 1868)
Archibald Scott Couper, Scottish chemist (advances in chemical theory including bonding of carbon atoms), born in Kirkintilloch, Scotland (d. 1892)
John La Farge, American painter (stained glass), born in New York City (d. 1910)
Robert Ross McBurney, Irish-born American 1st paid secretary of the YMCA (NYC), born in Ireland (d. 1898)
Léon Dierx, French poet (Aspirations; Les Amants), born in Saint-Denis, France (d. 1912)
John Herbert Kelly, American solider and the youngest Brigadier General in the Confederate Army, born in Carrollton, Alabama (d. 1864)
Andrew Lang, Scottish poet, novelist, literary critic, and anthropologist (Andrew Lang's Fairy Books), born in Selkirk, Scottish Borders, Scotland (d. 1912)
Jarolslaw Zielinski, Polish-American concert pianist, teacher, and composer, born in Lubycza Królewska, Poland (d. 1922)
Yegor Ivanovich Zolotarev, Russian mathematician (analysis and number theory), born in St Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1878)[1]
John Henry Roberts, Welsh organist, hymn composer, and pedagogue, born in  Pen-rallt, Caernarfonshire, Wales (d. 1924)
Dugald Clerk, Scottish engineer and inventor (1st 2-stroke motorcycle engine), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1932)
Alfred E. Hunt, founder of Alcoa (1888), the world's largest producer of aluminium, born in East Douglas, Massachusetts (d. 1899)
Isidor Traube, German chemist who founded capillary chemistry and researched liquids (osmosis, surface tension), born in Hildesheim Hanover (d. 1943)
Heinrich Rubens, German physicist (black-body rdiation), born in Wiesbaden (d. 1922)
Karl Bonhoeffer, German psychiatrist and neurologist (resisted Nazi's Aktion T4 extermination campaign), born in Neresheim, Württemberg (d. 1948)
Tommy Ryan, American boxer (World Welterweight, Middleweight champion), born in Redwood, NY (d. 1948)
Sergei Diaghilev, Russian arts patron and ballet impresario (founder of the Imperial Ballet and Ballets Russes), born in Gruzino Novgorod, Russia (d. 1929)
Henri Marteau, French violinist and composer, born in Reims, France (d. 1934)
Borisav "Bora" Stanković, Serbian writer of novels and short stories (Nečista Krv / Impure Blood), born in Vranje, Ottoman Empire (d. 1927)[1][2]
Adriaan van Maanen, Dutch-American astronomer, known for opposing the island universe hypothesis based on incorrect measurements, born in Sneek, Netherlands (d. 1946)[1]
Jules Pascin [Julius Pincas], Bulgarian-American painter and cartoonist, born in Vidin, Bulgaria (d. 1930)
Jose Maria Usandizaga, Spanish Basque composer (Mendi Mendiyan - High in the Mountains), born in Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain (d. 1915)
(William) Lawrence Bragg, English physicist (Nobel Prize for Physics, 1915 - crystalline structure analysis by means of X-rays), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 1971)[1]
Andy Varipapa, American ten-pin bowler (BPAA All-Star champion 1947-48), born in Carfizzi, Italy (d. 1984)
Erich Walter Sternberg, German-Israeli composer (co-founder of Israel Philharmonic Orchestra), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1974)
Ion Pillat, Romanian poet and senator (Umbra timpului), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1945)
Victor Varconi, Hungarian actor (Divine Lady; King of Kings; Big City), born in Kisvárda, Austria-Hungary (d. 1976)
Stanislav Maczek, Polish general, tank commander and war hero (WWII Battle of Falaise, Normandy invasion), born in Lwòw, Poland (d. 1994)
Clemens Krauss, Austrian conductor (Vienna State Opera; Berlin State Opera), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1954)
John Jay McCloy, American lawyer and banker (Secretary of War 1941-45, Chairman Chase Manhattan Bank, President of World Bank 1947-49), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1989)
Lizzie Miles [Elizabeth Landreaux], Creole American jazz, blues and gospel singer ("Man O'War"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1963)
Vardis Fisher, American author (Darkness & Deep), born in Annis, Idaho (d. 1968)
Zlatko Baloković, Croatian-American concert violinist, born in Zagreb, Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1955)
Henry, English Prince and Duke of Gloucester (son of King George V, Governor General of Australia 1945-47), born at Sandringham Estate, England (d. 1974)
Mule Suttles, American Baseball HOF infielder (5 x NgL All Star; NL batting champion 1926, 28; NgL Triple Crown 1926; St. Louis Stars), born in Edgewater, Alabama (d. 1966)
George Treweeke, Australian Rugby League second rower (7 Tests, South Sydney), born in Grahamstown, NSW (d. 1991)
Gerald Pearson, American physicist and inventor of the solar cell, born in Salem, Oregon (d. 1987)[1]
Lauri Saikkola, Finnish violinist (Viipuri Symphony, 1923–34; Helsinki Philharmonic, 1934–65), and composer (Laulusarja; Unen kaivo), born in Viipuri, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1995)
Eddie Quillan, American actor (Mutiny on the Bounty, Julia, Hell Town), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1990)
Red Norvo [Kenneth Norville], American jazz vibraphonist known as "Mr. Swing" ("Congo Blues"; "Knockin' On Wood"), born in Beardstown, Illinois (d. 1999)
Pieter "Piet" Jongeling, Dutch politician (GPV) and author of children's books (Scout: The Secret of the Swamp), born in Broeksterwâld, Netherlands (d. 1985)
Robert Brasillach, French author and journalist (editor in chief of the fascist weekly Je Suis Partout), born in Perpignan, France (d. 1945)
Elisabeth Grümmer, German soprano, Kammersängerin, and teacher, born in Niederjeutz, Alsace-Lorraine, German Empire (now Yutz, France) (d. 1986)
Freddie Green, American jazz guitarist (Count Basie Orchestra, 1937-87), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1987)
Dai Rees, Scottishgolfer(British Open runner-up 1953-54, 61), born in Fontegary, Glamorgan (d. 1983)
Etta Baker, American blues guitarist, born in Caldwell County, North Carolina (d. 2006)
Walter Winterbottom, England soccer manager (first manager England national football team 1946–62; FA Director of Coaching), born in Oldham, England (d. 2002)
Octavio Paz, Mexican poet (Salamandra; Topoemas), writer and diplomat who won the 1990 Nobel Prize laureate, born in Mexico City (d. 1998)
Eliseo Pajaro, Filipino composer (The Life of Lam-Ling; Marilag Fantasy), pedagogue, and school administrator, born in Badoc, Philippines (d. 1984)
Henry Morgan, American comedian and TV panelist (I've Got a Secret, Arena), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Jack Perry, English businessman and the 1st Western businessperson to visit Communist China, born in London (d. 1996)[1]
Shōichi Yokoi, Imperial Japanese Army sergeant, World War II (surrendered in 1972), born in Saori, Empire of Japan (d. 1997)
John H. Wood, Jr., American federal judge (US Western District of Texas, 1970-79), born in Rockport, Texas (d. 1979)
Lucille Bliss, American voice actress (Cinderella, The Secret of NIMH), born in New York City (d. 2012)
Tommy Bolt, American golfer (US Open 1958), born in Haworth, Oklahoma (d. 2008)
Helen Kemp (née Hubbert), American choral conductor, composer, and voice teacher, born in Perkasie, Pennsylvania (d. 2015)
